title: “There are these two extremes, monks, that one who has gone forth should not pursue: devotion to sensual pleasure in sensual objects — low, vulgar,…
content: “There are these two extremes, monks, that one who has gone forth should not pursue: devotion to sensual pleasure in sensual objects — low, vulgar, worldly, ignoble, unbeneficial; and devotion to self-mortification — painful, ignoble, unbeneficial. Avoiding these two extremes, monks, the Middle Way has been realized by the Tathāgata, giving rise to vision, giving rise to knowledge, leading to peace, direct knowledge, enlightenment, Nibbāna. And what, monks, is that Middle Way …? It is the Noble Eightfold Path, namely: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration. This, monks, is the Middle Way … leading to Nibbāna. This, monks, is the noble truth of suffering. Birth is suffering, aging is suffering, sickness is suffering, death is suffering, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair are suffering, association with the disliked is suffering, separation from the liked is suffering, not getting what one wants is suffering. In short, the five aggregates of clinging are suffering. This, monks, is the noble truth of the origin of suffering. It is this craving that leads to renewed existence, accompanied by delight and lust, delighting here and there — that is, craving for sensual pleasures, craving for existence, craving for non-existence. This, monks, is the noble truth of the cessation of suffering. It is the remainderless fading away and cessation of that same craving, the abandoning and relinquishing of it, freedom from it, non-reliance. This, monks, is the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. It is the Noble Eightfold Path, namely: right view … right concentration.”

title: “Venerable sir, what is the first jhāna?” “Here, venerable sir, a monk, secluded from sensual desires, secluded from unwholesome states, enters and dwells in the…
content: “Venerable sir, what is the first jhāna?” “Here, venerable sir, a monk, secluded from sensual desires, secluded from unwholesome states, enters and dwells in the first jhāna, accompanied by initial and sustained thought, with rapture and pleasure born of seclusion. This, venerable sir, is called the first jhāna.” “Venerable sir, how many factors does the first jhāna have?” “Venerable sir, the first jhāna has five factors. Here, a monk in the first jhāna has initial thought, sustained thought, rapture, pleasure, and unification of mind. Thus, venerable sir, the first jhāna has five factors.” “Venerable sir, how many factors does the first jhāna abandon and how many does it possess?” “Venerable sir, the first jhāna abandons five factors and possesses five factors. Here, a monk in the first jhāna has abandoned sensual desire, abandoned ill-will, abandoned sloth and torpor, abandoned restlessness and remorse, abandoned doubt; he possesses initial thought, sustained thought, rapture, pleasure, and unification of mind. Thus, venerable sir, the first jhāna abandons five factors and possesses five factors.”

title: “Poṭṭhapāda, when defiling states are eliminated, pure states increase; here and now, by direct knowledge, one realizes and abides in fully developed, vast wisdom. Then…
content: “Poṭṭhapāda, when defiling states are eliminated, pure states increase; here and now, by direct knowledge, one realizes and abides in fully developed, vast wisdom. Then joy arises, gladness, tranquility, mindfulness, clear comprehension, and a pleasant abiding arise.”

title: “And, lord of gods, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk does…
content: “And, lord of gods, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk does not delight in those forms, does not praise them, and dwells without lust for them — then, because he does not delight, does not praise, and dwells without lust, not depending on them, consciousness arises without clinging to them. Without clinging, lord of gods, that monk attains Nibbāna. There are sounds cognizable by the ear … odors … tastes … touches … Lord of gods, there are phenomena cognizable by the mind that are desirable … enticing. If a monk does not delight in those phenomena, does not praise them, and dwells without lust for them — then, because he does not delight, does not praise, and dwells without lust, not depending on them, consciousness arises without clinging to them. Without clinging, lord of gods, that monk attains Nibbāna. This, lord of gods, is the cause, this is the reason why some beings here and now attain Nibbāna.”

title: “Udāyi, here some foolish person, when I say ‘Abandon this,’ says: ‘What is this trivial, petty thing? This ascetic is too strict about this restraint…
content: “Udāyi, here some foolish person, when I say ‘Abandon this,’ says: ‘What is this trivial, petty thing? This ascetic is too strict about this restraint and elimination.’ They do not abandon it and they create discontent against me and against the monks who wish to train in the precepts. That, Udāyi, becomes for them a strong bond, a firm bond, a sturdy bond, a rotting not-less bond, a great log.”

title: “Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk does not delight in…
content: “Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk does not delight in those forms, does not praise them, and dwells without lust for them — then that monk is called one who has not swallowed Māra’s hook, who has broken the hook, destroyed the hook, who has not fallen into misfortune, into calamity, not subject to the Evil One’s will. There are sounds … odors … tastes … touches … phenomena cognizable by the mind … If a monk does not delight in those phenomena, does not praise them, and dwells without lust for them — then that monk is called one who has not swallowed Māra’s hook, who has broken the hook, destroyed the hook, who has not fallen into misfortune, into calamity, not subject to the Evil One’s will.”

title: “Fear, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. Suffering, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. Disease, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. A…
content:
“Fear, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. Suffering, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. Disease, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. A boil, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. A dart, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. Bondage, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. Mud, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures. A womb, monks, is a synonym for sensual pleasures.
How, monks, is fear a synonym for sensual pleasures? Because those who are infected by sensual desire, bound by sensual lust, are not freed from fear in the present life, not freed from fear in the future — therefore fear is a synonym for sensual pleasures.”

title: Elder Uttiya: “Hearing a sound, mindfulness is lost — Attending to a pleasing sign, The mind feels infatuation, Clinging to the perception remains. The defilements…
content:
Elder Uttiya:
“Hearing a sound, mindfulness is lost —
Attending to a pleasing sign,
The mind feels infatuation,
Clinging to the perception remains.
The defilements increase,
Leading to defilement in saṃsāra.”

title: “And what, monks, is the peril in the case of forms? Here, monks, one might see that woman at another time — eighty, ninety, or…
content: “And what, monks, is the peril in the case of forms? Here, monks, one might see that woman at another time — eighty, ninety, or a hundred years old — aged, bent like a roof, crooked, leaning on a staff, trembling as she walks, sick, her youth exhausted, teeth broken, hair grey, bald, wrinkled, limbs shriveled and pale. What do you think, monks? Has her former charm and beauty disappeared and peril become evident?” “Yes, venerable sir.” “Thus, monks, is the peril in the case of forms.”

title: “Monks, what is a course of practice that is painful in the present but yields pleasant results in the future? Here, monks, a person by…
content: “Monks, what is a course of practice that is painful in the present but yields pleasant results in the future? Here, monks, a person by nature has strong lust, and he constantly experiences painful and grievous feelings born of lust. By nature he has strong ill-will, and constantly experiences painful and grievous feelings born of ill-will. By nature he has strong delusion, and constantly experiences painful and grievous feelings born of delusion. With pain and grief, with tearful face and crying, he lives the holy life that is fully complete and pure. At the breakup of the body, after death, he is reborn in a good destination, a heavenly world, in this realm. This, monks, is called a course of practice that is painful in the present but yields pleasant results in the future.”
